questions.question: Explain the HttpServletRequest interface and list any four of its commonly used methods.
questions.hint: Focus on methods that retrieve request data like parameters, headers, and HTTP method
keyPoints: HttpServletRequest is an interface that extends ServletRequest
It represents an HTTP request sent by the client to the servlet
Provides access to request parameters, headers, and method information
Methods: getParameter(), getMethod(), getHeader(), getSession(), getRequestURI(), getQueryString()
Used to retrieve client data submitted through HTML forms

questions.question: Explain the HttpServletResponse interface and state its significance in servlet programming.
questions.hint: Remember to mention both header and content management methods
keyPoints: HttpServletResponse extends ServletResponse interface
Represents the HTTP response sent from servlet to client
Provides methods to set response status, headers, and content type
Key methods: setContentType(), setStatus(), getWriter(), getOutputStream(), addCookie(), sendRedirect()
Enables servlet to send dynamic content back to the browser

questions.question: What is HttpSession? Explain any three methods of HttpSession interface.
questions.hint: Session management is crucial for maintaining user state in web applications
keyPoints: HttpSession represents a session between the client and server
Used to maintain user state across multiple HTTP requests
getAttribute(): Returns object bound to specified name
setAttribute(): Binds an object to the session with a given name
invalidate(): Invalidates the session and removes all bound objects
getId(): Returns unique session identifier

questions.question: Explain the Cookie class in jakarta.servlet.http package and its commonly used methods.
questions.hint: Cookies are added to response using addCookie() method of HttpServletResponse
keyPoints: Cookie is a small piece of data stored on the client's browser
Used for session tracking and maintaining user preferences
Constructor: Cookie(String name, String value)
getName(): Returns the cookie name
getValue(): Returns the cookie value
setMaxAge(): Sets the duration for which cookie persists
setPath(): Specifies the URL path for which cookie is valid

questions.question: Compare doGet() and doPost() methods of HttpServlet class.
questions.hint: Understanding the difference is essential for proper form handling
keyPoints: doGet(): Handles GET requests - data sent in query string, limited length, visible in URL
doPost(): Handles POST requests - data sent in request body, larger data, not visible in URL
GET is idempotent, POST is non-idempotent
GET is faster but less secure, POST is slower but more secure
Use GET for retrieving data, POST for submitting sensitive data
Both methods receive HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse parameters

questions.question: Explain the purpose of HttpServlet class in the Jakarta Servlet API.
questions.hint: HttpServlet is the base class for most web servlets
keyPoints: HttpServlet is an abstract class that extends GenericServlet
Provides a framework for handling HTTP requests
Overridden methods: doGet(), doPost(), doPut(), doDelete(), doHead(), doOptions()
service() method dispatches HTTP requests to appropriate doXXX() methods
Developers typically extend HttpServlet to create servlets
Handles HTTP-specific features like cookies, sessions, and headers

questions.question: Explain in detail the HttpServletRequest interface with its important methods for handling HTTP requests.
questions.hint: Group methods by their functionality for better explanation
keyPoints: HttpServletRequest extends ServletRequest interface
Represents client request received by the servlet
Method retrieval: getMethod(), getRequestURI(), getProtocol()
Parameter handling: getParameter(), getParameterValues(), getParameterMap()
Header information: getHeader(), getHeaders(), getHeaderNames()
Session management: getSession(), getSession(false)
Cookie retrieval: getCookies()
Request dispatching: getRequestDispatcher()
Input stream: getInputStream(), getReader()

questions.question: Explain in detail the HttpServletResponse interface with its important methods for sending HTTP responses.
questions.hint: Explain both header manipulation and content output methods
keyPoints: HttpServletResponse extends ServletResponse interface
Represents the response sent from servlet to client
Content type: setContentType(), getContentType()
Status codes: setStatus(), sendError(), SC_OK, SC_NOT_FOUND, SC_INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR
Headers: setHeader(), addHeader(), setDateHeader(), addIntHeader()
Output: getWriter() for character output, getOutputStream() for binary output
Redirecting: sendRedirect() for URL redirection
Cookies: addCookie() to send cookies to client
Buffering: setBufferSize(), getBufferSize(), flushBuffer()

questions.question: Discuss the various methods of HttpSession interface used for session management in servlets.
questions.hint: Session management is essential for maintaining user state
keyPoints: HttpSession provides server-side session tracking
getAttribute(String name): Returns bound attribute object
setAttribute(String name, Object value): Binds object to session
removeAttribute(String name): Removes bound attribute
getId(): Returns unique session identifier
getCreationTime(): Returns session creation timestamp
getLastAccessedTime(): Returns last client access time
getMaxInactiveInterval(): Returns session timeout period
setMaxInactiveInterval(int seconds): Sets session timeout
invalidate(): Invalidates session and releases all objects
isNew(): Returns true if session is new and not acknowledged by client

questions.question: Compare HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse interfaces, highlighting their differences and roles.
questions.hint: Focus on their complementary roles in request-response cycle
keyPoints: HttpServletRequest represents incoming client request
HttpServletResponse represents outgoing server response
Request is read-only from servlet perspective; response is write-only
Request methods: getParameter(), getHeader(), getMethod()
Response methods: setContentType(), getWriter(), addCookie()
Request accesses client data submitted to servlet
Response sends data back to client (HTML, JSON, files)
Both are interfaces extending their generic counterparts
Both are created by the servlet container and passed to servlet service method

questions.question: Explain the lifecycle of a servlet with respect to HTTP requests, detailing the role of jakarta.servlet.http package classes.
questions.hint: Connect lifecycle phases with jakarta.servlet.http package components
keyPoints: Loading and instantiation: Servlet class is loaded and instance created
Initialization: init() method called once, receives ServletConfig
Request handling: service() method receives HttpServletRequest and HttpServletResponse
service() dispatches to doGet(), doPost() based on HTTP method
HttpServletRequest provides access to all request data
HttpServletResponse handles building the response
HttpSession manages user sessions across requests
Cookies are managed through HttpServletResponse.addCookie()
Destruction: destroy() called once when servlet is unloaded

questions.question: Write a servlet code to demonstrate handling a GET request that reads a username parameter and displays a personalized greeting.
questions.hint: Remember to set proper content type and handle null parameters
keyPoints: Import jakarta.servlet.http.* packages
Extend HttpServlet class
Override doGet() method
Get PrintWriter from response object
Set content type to text/html
Use request.getParameter() to read username
Handle null/empty parameter case
Print HTML response with greeting
Use try-catch for ServletException and IOException

questions.question: Write a servlet code to demonstrate session management by tracking the number of visits by a user.
questions.hint: Handle both new sessions and existing sessions properly
keyPoints: Import jakarta.servlet.http.* packages
Extend HttpServlet and override doGet()
Get existing session using request.getSession()
Retrieve visit count using session.getAttribute('visitCount')
Initialize visit count to 1 if null
Increment visit count for subsequent visits
Store updated count using session.setAttribute()
Display visit count to user
Optionally show session ID using session.getId()

questions.question: Write a servlet code to demonstrate setting and retrieving cookies using jakarta.servlet.http package.
questions.hint: Handle null array when no cookies are present
keyPoints: Import jakarta.servlet.http.Cookie class
Create Cookie object with name and value: new Cookie('username', 'john')
Set cookie properties: setMaxAge(), setPath()
Add cookie to response: response.addCookie(cookie)
Retrieve cookies: Cookie[] cookies = request.getCookies()
Loop through cookies to find desired one using getName()
Extract value using getValue()
Handle case when no cookies exist
Return appropriate response to client
